beginner.coaching: Grabbing the pawn with Qxb2 pulls your queen away to the edge and wastes a move while your pieces are still sitting at home — you're already doing very well, so there's no need to go hunting for a small snack. Instead, play Qxd2, trading queens right off the board; your queen on b4 can capture the enemy queen on d2, and after they recapture you've simplified into a comfortable position. From there you can develop pieces like the bishop and get your king safe. Simplifying when you're ahead makes the game much easier to convert.
beginner.takeaway: When you're ahead, trade queens and finish developing rather than chasing pawns.

advanced.coaching: You grabbed the pawn on b2, but with your king still stuck in the center and pieces undeveloped, chasing material with the queen just sends her wandering while White catches up. The cleanest solution here is Qxd2, trading queens right off the board: you swap your active queen for White's, and after they recapture you can play Bd6 to develop and head toward castling. When you're better developed and safely ahead, simplifying into the endgame removes all the counterplay your opponent might dream up. If you preferred keeping pieces on, Nc6 was also excellent — it develops, hits the pawn on d4, and shores up your queen.

beginner.coaching: I'd play Qxd2. Qxb2 is understandable because it attacks the pawn on c2, but it lets White recapture with Nbxd2. Play Qxd2 instead: your queen takes the queen on d2, so White’s biggest loose piece is gone, and you have also attacked the pawn on c2.
beginner.takeaway: When you can take a loose piece, do it with your queen; if the piece is defended, look for another capture or development.

intermediate.coaching: I'd play Qxd2. Qxb2 is understandable because it removes the hanging pawn, but it lets White recapture on c2 and keeps the pressure on d4. Play Qxd2 instead: your queen recaptures the hanging queen, and after ...Nd2, you can recapture on c2 and keep the pressure on d4.
intermediate.takeaway: When you can recapture a hanging queen while keeping pressure on the center, do that over recapturing a loose pawn.

advanced.coaching: I'd play Qxd2. Qxb2 is understandable because it removes the hanging pawn, but it lets White recapture on c2 and keeps the pressure on d4. advanced.takeaway: When you can recapture a hanging piece while attacking another loose piece, do that over recapturing the same hanging pawn again.

intermediate.coaching: I’d choose Ne2. Your d4 idea leaves the tension unresolved, but Ne2 develops a minor piece while also defending your queen on c1 and your loose pawn on g3, so it improves your position without creating a new target. A simple plan is Ne2, then keep developing and only open the center when your pieces are better placed.

beginner.coaching: A good practical move here is dxe4. It removes Black’s loose pawn on e4, and after the capture your pawn also attacks the knight on d5 and the pawn on f5, so you are not just defending — you are gaining time with threats. Compared with d4, this is a more forcing beginner habit: when a central pawn is available to capture safely, look at that before pushing past it.
beginner.takeaway: In the center, prefer capturing a loose pawn when it also creates threats, rather than pushing by automatically.

intermediate.coaching: Playing d4 pushed past the tension and left your center loose while giving Black a free hand. Instead, try Ne2, which develops your last minor piece toward the action and quietly shores up your position — it covers both your queen on c1 and the weak pawn on g3. That's the real lesson here: before pushing pawns, get your pieces working and defend your soft spots. After Ne2 you can follow up naturally with your bishop swinging to c3, aiming right at Black's kingside.

Play dxe4 instead: you capture the pawn on e4 and, at the same time, your capture attacks both the knight on d5 and the pawn on f5. That's the beauty of capturing toward the center — it opens lines and lets one pawn hit two Black pieces at once. When you have a pawn tension like this, take a moment to see who benefits from resolving it before shoving a pawn forward.

advanced.coaching: I'd play Be1. Your ...d4 is understandable because it attacks the knight on d5, but it does not solve the bigger problem: your queen on c1 is attacked by Black’s ...Nf6, and your pawn on g3 is defended only by your rook on a1. Be1 is the cleaner move: it defends the queen while also attacking the knight on d5, so Black cannot use ...Nf6 to gain time on your queen.

This keeps you in charge of the only open file rather than chasing shadows with a check.
advanced.takeaway: A check is only worth playing if it actually improves your position — otherwise use your doubled rooks to win the open file by trading, not by giving harmless checks.

beginner.coaching: I'd play Kc3. Your a4 pawn is tempting because it defends b5, but it does not solve the bigger problem: White’s king is still on c3, and your pawn on c3 is hanging. Play Kc3 instead: your king moves off c3, attacks the hanging pawn, and your next job is to recapture on c3.

intermediate.coaching: I'd play Kc4. a4 is understandable because it defends b5, but it lets White play g7 and keep pressure on your center. A cleaner move is Kc4: your king defends b5 while also defending c5, so White cannot simply recapture on c5.
intermediate.takeaway: When you are defending a center pawn, choose the square that also defends another loose center square.

Your knight on d5 was doing great work in the center, so pulling it back to b4 gave away its strong post — and it can be chased around from there. Instead, jump into f4 with that same knight. From f4 it hits three of White's pawns at once (d3, g2, and h3), turning your active knight into an immediate source of threats rather than a piece being pushed backward.

Takeaway: When you have a well-placed knight, look for a square that keeps it active and creates threats, rather than retreating it.

beginner.coaching: Your knight on d5 was sitting where it could be chased, so moving it was the right instinct — but Nf4 is the stronger choice. From f4 your knight jumps to an active square where it attacks three of White's pawns at once, on d3, g2, and h3, putting real pressure on his position. Compare that to Ndb4, where your knight ends up on the edge doing far less; a knight in the center or near the enemy king does more work than one shoved to the side.

beginner.coaching: I'd play Nf4. Ndb4 is a normal developing move, but it lets White answer with ...Ba4, defending the knight on c6 and keeping pressure on d3. Play Nf4 instead: your knight attacks d3 and g2, and if White recaptures with ...Ba4, you have traded off the c6 defender.
beginner.takeaway: When you can attack a loose piece, do so; if the opponent defends it, look for a recapture that removes the defender.

intermediate.coaching: I'd play Nf4. Ndb4 is a very natural developing move because it defends c6 and attacks d3, but it leaves your knight on c6 undefended. Play Nf4 instead: it attacks d3 and g2 while defending c6, so your knight is no longer a loose piece.
intermediate.takeaway: When your piece is attacked, choose the developing move that defends it while attacking the loose target.

advanced.coaching: I'd play Nf4. Ndb4 is a very natural developing move because it defends c6 and attacks d3, but it leaves your knight on c6 hanging while White’s bishop on d3 is still attacking it. Play Nf4 instead: it attacks d3 and g2, keeps c6 defended, and if White recaptures with Ba4, you can continue with ...Nb4, putting pressure on the c-file and keeping your pieces coordinated.
advanced.takeaway: When an attacked piece is defended, look for a developing move that attacks the same target while defending the defender.

beginner.coaching: The move to look for is Bc6+. Instead of grabbing the knight on a6, your bishop gives check and also keeps attacking the rook on a8, so Black must answer the check while your bishop stays active. This is a great beginner habit: when you can make a forcing move with tempo, check it before taking material.
beginner.takeaway: Checks that also attack something are often stronger than immediate captures.

advanced.coaching: Bc6+ was the move to look for: instead of grabbing the knight on a6, you use your active bishop to give check while also attacking the rook on a8. This is stronger because the check forces Black to respond first, so your next move can be about winning material rather than hoping Black gives you time. A simple sample is Bc6+ Qd7 Bxd7+.
advanced.takeaway: When you can make a forcing move with tempo, check first before taking loose material.

beginner.coaching: Your rook move to e1 was reasonable in spirit, but it left your other rook without help and gave up the idea of pressuring Black's center. A cleaner plan is Rad1, bringing your rook to the d-file where it immediately attacks the pawn on d6, and it also defends your rook on f1 so both rooks are working together. If Black tries to grab your bishop with the knight, you simply recapture with your knight and keep a healthy position. This teaches you to put rooks on files where they create pressure while keeping your pieces defending each other.

beginner.coaching: In this kind of pawn race, your king is your most important piece, so use it to protect your passed pawn. By staying connected with Kg5, your king can escort the f-pawn and stay involved.

beginner.coaching: Your instinct to grab the pawn on h6 with Rh8 isn't crazy, but in these rook endings activity matters more than snatching a pawn. Try Rc7 instead: it keeps your rook active and, if the black king steps forward, you get a useful check to keep the king from settling in. The key idea is that your rook should be doing something forcing rather than parked on the edge going after material.
beginner.takeaway: In rook endings, prize an active, checking rook over winning a single pawn.

intermediate.coaching: Your rook on h7 was hanging next to the black king, so you were right to sense it needed to move — but Rh8 keeps the rook a bit passive and tied to grabbing a pawn. A cleaner idea is Rc7, getting the rook active on an open line; after the king steps to g5 you have Rc5+, checking the king and using your rook to harass it from the side. That side-checking motif is exactly the technique you want in these rook-and-pawn races, since it slows the enemy king and keeps your rook working.
intermediate.takeaway: In rook endings, activate your rook with checks from the side rather than chasing a single pawn.

The pawn push, by contrast, just gave up your advanced pawn without developing anything or improving your attack. When you have a queen already active, add attackers with check and tempo rather than lunging pawns forward.

Takeaway: Prefer moves that develop and give check together — they gain time and keep the initiative on your side.

The point is that developing with tempo — check plus a capture — is far stronger than advancing a pawn that isn't actually accomplishing anything yet; your bishop comes into the game and stays active.
advanced.takeaway: When you can capture a loose piece with check, that forcing developing move usually beats a slow pawn push.

Instead, try f5, pushing your pawn forward to strike at Black's pawns on e6 and g6. This opens lines toward the Black king in the center and creates real pressure rather than just trading pieces. When you're under some strain, an active pawn break that opens the game often gives you more counterplay than a quiet capture.

intermediate.coaching: Capturing the bishop with Nxd3 is natural, but it trades off your only active knight and hands Black an easy recapture — you end up giving away your best-placed piece for nothing extra. Instead, I'd play f5, striking at Black's pawns on e6 and g6 and opening lines toward the enemy king that's still stuck in the center. After f5 exf5 you can tuck your king to safety with Kb2, keeping your pieces working together. The idea is to create threats against the uncastled king rather than rushing an exchange that helps your opponent.
intermediate.takeaway: Before trading a strong piece, look for an active pawn break that opens the position against a king still stuck in the center.

beginner.coaching: I’d play g5. Instead of using the f-pawn to attack the bishop on g2, this move makes your structure sturdier by defending the pawn on f4, so White has fewer easy targets to aim at. For a beginner, that is a very reusable habit: when you are ahead in material or have dangerous pawns, make them protect each other before starting a chase.
beginner.takeaway: Before attacking an opponent’s piece, check whether you can first improve and defend your own pawns.

advanced.coaching: I’d prefer 1...h5. Your 1...f3 attacks the bishop on g2 right away, but it commits that pawn; h5 is the more patient move, improving your position while keeping choices like Qf6 or g5 available later. Since nothing is loose, you don’t need to force contact immediately.
advanced.takeaway: When there is no urgent tactic, improve first and keep your pawn breaks flexible.

advanced.coaching: Pushing f3 looks tempting because it kicks the bishop, but after it retreats to h3 your pawn on f3 is far from home and your king is a bit airy, so you've traded your active advantage for a loose structure. Instead, play h5 to give your king a safe breathing square and keep everything solid before you press. That way your queen stays active on c3 and you can follow with something like Qf6, coordinating your pieces without creating new weaknesses.
advanced.takeaway: When you're better, improve your king's safety and keep your structure intact before lunging with pawn thrusts that can't be supported.

intermediate.coaching: Pushing the pawn to f3 looks aggressive, but it attacks the bishop on g2 without any real follow-up — after the bishop simply steps to h3 your pawn is stuck deep in enemy territory with no support, and you've given away the tension for nothing. Instead, try h5, keeping your pawns connected and your position solid while you look for a better moment to make progress. The key difference is that h5 improves your structure and doesn't commit to a lunge that can be brushed aside.
intermediate.takeaway: Before pushing a pawn to attack a piece, make sure you have a real threat behind it — otherwise you just hand your opponent a free tempo to sidestep.
